questions.prompt: Which three triangle centers lie on the Euler line?
hints: Centroid, circumcenter, orthocenter — NOT the incenter (in general).
Euler discovered this in 1765.
questions.explanation: The Euler line contains centroid $G$, circumcenter $O$, and orthocenter $H$ — but NOT the incenter (in general).

questions.prompt: On the Euler line, what is the ratio OG : GH?
hints: G is one-third of the way from O to H.
So OG is 1, GH is 2.
questions.explanation: $OG : GH = 1 : 2$. The centroid divides $OH$ in ratio $1:2$, closer to the circumcenter.

questions.prompt: In which case does the Euler line degenerate (collapse to a point)?
hints: All centers coincide in this special case.
Maximum symmetry.
questions.explanation: Equilateral triangle: all four classical centers coincide, so the Euler 'line' is degenerate (a single point).

questions.promptTex: \text{If } O = (1, 2) \text{ and } H = (7, 14), \text{ find } G \text{ (on the Euler line).}
\text{In an isosceles triangle, the incenter } I \text{ relative to the Euler line:}
choices: Always lies off the Euler line
hints: Isosceles triangles have an axis of symmetry.
All four centers lie on that axis.
questions.explanation: In an isosceles triangle, all four classical centers ($I, G, O, H$) lie on the axis of symmetry — which IS the Euler line. In a scalene triangle, the incenter generally is NOT on the Euler line.

hints: $G = O + (1/3)(H - O) \;\Longrightarrow\; O = G - (1/2)(H - G)$.
Or: $O$ is on Euler line such that $OG = (1/2) GH$, opposite direction from $H$.

steps.text: Or: $O$ is on Euler line such that $OG = (1/2) GH$, opposite direction from $H$.

questions.prompt: Where does the nine-point center N sit on the Euler line?
choices: Between G and H (1/3 of the way from G)
hints: N is the center of the nine-point circle.
It's the midpoint of segment OH on the Euler line.
questions.explanation: The nine-point center N is at the midpoint of $OH$. So on the Euler line in order: $O, N, G, H$ with ratios $0 : 3 : 2 : 6$ (when measured from $O$).

hints: G divides OH in ratio 1:2 from O.

questions.promptTex: \text{The Euler relation in vector form is:}
hints: From $OG : GH = 1 : 2$ we get $G = O + (1/3)(H - O)$. Rearrange for $H$.
questions.explanation: $\vec{H} = 3\vec{G} - 2\vec{O}$. Derivation: $G$ is one-third of the way from $O$ to $H$, so $G = O + (1/3)(H - O)$. Multiply by 3: $3G = 3O + H - O = 2O + H$. Rearrange: $H = 3G - 2O$. This is Euler's vector identity — among the most elegant in classical geometry.
steps: **Start from the ratio.** $G$ divides $OH$ in ratio $1:2$, so $\vec{OG} = \dfrac{1}{3} \vec{OH}$, i.e. $\vec{G} - \vec{O} = \dfrac{1}{3}(\vec{H} - \vec{O})$.
**Solve for $\vec{H}$.** Multiply both sides by 3: $3\vec{G} - 3\vec{O} = \vec{H} - \vec{O}$. Move $\vec{O}$ over: $\vec{H} = 3\vec{G} - 2\vec{O}$.
**Equivalent forms.** $\vec{O} = (3\vec{G} - \vec{H})/2$, or $\vec{G} = (2\vec{O} + \vec{H})/3$. The same identity rewritten to solve for each center.

**Why this matters.** With $\vec{G} = (\vec{A} + \vec{B} + \vec{C})/3$, the identity also gives $\vec{H} = \vec{A} + \vec{B} + \vec{C} - 2\vec{O}$ — a clean expression of the orthocenter in terms of the vertices and circumcenter.

questions.prompt: Why are O, G, and H always collinear in any (non-equilateral) triangle?
choices: Coincidence — works only for special triangles
A direct vector computation: $H = A + B + C - 2O$, so $H - 3G = A + B + C - 2O - (A + B + C) = -2O$, giving $H + 2O = 3G$. That linear relation FORCES O, G, H onto a line
Because the centroid is the midpoint of OH
hints: Three points are collinear ⇔ one is a linear combination of the other two.
$H + 2O = 3G$ is exactly such a relation — it says $G$ is an affine combination of $O$ and $H$.
questions.explanation: The vector identity $H + 2O = 3G$ (equivalently $G = \dfrac{2O + H}{3}$) means $G$ is a fixed affine combination of $O$ and $H$. Any point that's a convex combination of two others lies on the segment between them — so $G$ lies on segment $OH$. Collinearity is forced by the linear relation; not coincidence.
steps: **Set up.** Let $\vec{A}, \vec{B}, \vec{C}$ be the vertex position vectors, $\vec{O}$ the circumcenter.

**Key identity.** $\vec{H} = \vec{A} + \vec{B} + \vec{C} - 2\vec{O}$. (Can be derived from the orthocenter's defining property using $\vec{OA} \cdot \vec{BC} = 0$ for each pair, expanded coordinate-by-coordinate.)

**Compute $\vec{H} - 3\vec{G}$.** Substituting $\vec{G} = (\vec{A} + \vec{B} + \vec{C})/3$, so $3\vec{G} = \vec{A} + \vec{B} + \vec{C}$. Then $\vec{H} - 3\vec{G} = -2\vec{O}$, i.e. $\vec{H} + 2\vec{O} = 3\vec{G}$.
**Rearrange.** $\vec{G} = \dfrac{2\vec{O} + \vec{H}}{3}$. $G$ is an affine combination of $O$ and $H$ → all three are collinear, with $G$ on segment $OH$.

**Read off the ratio.** $G$ is at weight $\dfrac{2}{3}$ from $O$? No — the formula says $G$ is at parameter $1/3$ along $OH$ (since the coefficient of $H$ is $1/3$). So $OG : OH = 1 : 3$, i.e. $OG : GH = 1 : 2$. Matches Euler's ratio.

**Pattern lesson.** Collinearity proofs in geometry often reduce to finding an affine relation $\alpha \vec{P_1} + \beta \vec{P_2} + \gamma \vec{P_3} = \vec{0}$ with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 0$. Here the relation is $\vec{H} - 3\vec{G} + 2\vec{O} = \vec{0}$ with $1 - 3 + 2 = 0$ ✓.

questions.promptTex: \text{Triangle with vertices } A(0,0), B(4,0), C(1,3). \\ \text{All three of } O, G, H \text{ have the SAME y-coordinate (the Euler line is horizontal). What is it?}
hints: Find $O$: perpendicular bisector of $AB$ is $x = 2$; perpendicular bisector of $AC$ gives $y = 1$ at that $x$. So $O = (2, 1)$.
Find $H$ using $H = 3G - 2O = (5,3) - (4,2) = (1, 1)$. All three at $y = 1$.
questions.explanation: Compute each center: $G = (5/3, 1)$ (average of vertices), $O = (2, 1)$ (intersection of perpendicular bisectors), $H = 3G - 2O = (1, 1)$ (via the Euler relation). All have $y = 1$ — the Euler line is the horizontal $y = 1$.
steps: **Centroid first.** $G = \left(\dfrac{0+4+1}{3}, \dfrac{0+0+3}{3}\right) = (5/3, 1)$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="40,170 200,170 80,50" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="15" y1="130" x2="235" y2="130" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="6 4"/><circle cx="120" cy="130" r="4" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="107" cy="130" r="4" fill="#16a34a"/><circle cx="80" cy="130" r="4" fill="#dc2626"/><circle cx="40" cy="170" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="200" cy="170" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="80" cy="50" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="24" y="186" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A(0,0)</text><text x="180" y="186" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B(4,0)</text><text x="68" y="42" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C(1,3)</text><text x="125" y="125" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#2563eb">O</text><text x="100" y="122" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#16a34a">G</text><text x="73" y="122" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#dc2626">H</text><text x="30" y="196" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">Euler line is the horizontal line y = 1</text></svg>

steps: **Circumcenter.** $AB$ is on the x-axis from $(0,0)$ to $(4,0)$, perpendicular bisector: $x = 2$. Bisector of $AC$ from midpoint $(0.5, 1.5)$: $AC$ slope $= 3$, perpendicular slope $= -1/3$. Line: $y - 1.5 = -\dfrac{1}{3}(x - 0.5)$. At $x = 2$: $y = 1.5 - \dfrac{1}{3}(1.5) = 1$. So $O = (2, 1)$.
**Orthocenter via Euler identity.** $\vec{H} = 3\vec{G} - 2\vec{O} = 3(5/3, 1) - 2(2, 1) = (5, 3) - (4, 2) = (1, 1)$.
**Conclusion.** All three centers have $y = 1$. The Euler line is $y = 1$.

hints: $G$ divides $OH$ in ratio $1:2$, so $|GH| = (2/3) |OH|$.
steps: **Use the ratio.** $OG : GH = 1 : 2$, so $OG = \dfrac{1}{3} OH$ and $GH = \dfrac{2}{3} OH$.
**Verify by summing.** $OG + GH = 4 + 8 = 12 = OH$ ✓.

questions.prompt: Order the four points O (circumcenter), G (centroid), N (nine-point center), H (orthocenter) along the Euler line, starting from O.
hints: $G$ is at fraction $1/3$ along $OH$; $N$ is at fraction $1/2$ along $OH$.
$1/3 < 1/2 < 1$, so the order from O is G, then N, then H.
questions.explanation: Parametrize along $OH$ with $O$ at $0$ and $H$ at $1$. Then $G$ is at $1/3$ (Euler ratio) and $N$ is at $1/2$ (midpoint of $OH$). Since $0 < 1/3 < 1/2 < 1$, the order is $O, G, N, H$ — with segment ratios $OG : GN : NH = 2 : 1 : 3$.
steps: **Locate each point as a fraction of $OH$.** $O$ at $0$. $H$ at $1$. $G$ at $1/3$ (by the Euler ratio $OG:GH = 1:2$). $N$ is the midpoint of $OH$, so at $1/2$.
**Sort.** $0 < 1/3 < 1/2 < 1$, so the order from $O$ is $O, G, N, H$.
**Compute segment ratios.** $OG = 1/3$, $GN = 1/2 - 1/3 = 1/6$, $NH = 1 - 1/2 = 1/2$. Multiply by 6 to clear fractions: $OG : GN : NH = 2 : 1 : 3$.

**Pattern lesson.** $G$ is closer to $O$ than to $H$. $N$ sits exactly between them. The Euler line carries a lot of structure — there's also the de Longchamps point, the exeter point, and many other 'special points' all on the same line.

questions.promptTex: \text{Given } O = (1, 2) \text{ and } H = (7, 14), \\ \text{find the x-coordinate of the nine-point center } N.
hints: $N$ is the midpoint of $OH$.
questions.explanation: The nine-point center is the midpoint of $OH$: $N = \left(\dfrac{1+7}{2}, \dfrac{2+14}{2}\right) = (4, 8)$. So $x_N = 4$.
steps: **Recall the definition.** The nine-point center $N$ is the midpoint of segment $OH$ on the Euler line.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="20" y1="100" x2="260" y2="100" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="6 4"/><circle cx="40" cy="100" r="5" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="120" cy="100" r="5" fill="#16a34a"/><circle cx="160" cy="100" r="5" fill="#b45309"/><circle cx="240" cy="100" r="5" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="34" y="86" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="#2563eb">O</text><text x="114" y="86" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="#16a34a">G</text><text x="154" y="86" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="#b45309">N</text><text x="234" y="86" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="#dc2626">H</text><text x="70" y="125" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">1/3</text><text x="135" y="125" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">1/6</text><text x="195" y="125" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">1/2</text><text x="60" y="160" font-size="12" fill="#0f172a" font-style="italic">order along the Euler line: O, G, N, H</text></svg>

steps: **Compute the midpoint of $OH$.** $N = \left(\dfrac{1+7}{2}, \dfrac{2+14}{2}\right) = (4, 8)$.
**Report $x_N$.** $x_N = 4$.
**Cross-check with $G$.** $G = O + (1/3)(H - O) = (1, 2) + (2, 4) = (3, 6)$. As expected, $G$ comes before $N$ (since $1/3 < 1/2$) on the line from $O$ to $H$.

questions.prompt: The nine-point circle of a triangle has radius equal to:
hints: The nine-point circle passes through nine special points — including the three midpoints of the sides.
It's a scaled-down version of the circumcircle, by a factor of 1/2.
questions.explanation: The nine-point circle has radius $R/2$ — half the circumradius. Geometrically it's the scaled-down image of the circumcircle, under a *homothety* centered at $H$ with factor $1/2$. It passes through nine special points: the three side midpoints, the three altitude feet, and the three midpoints of segments from each vertex to $H$.
steps: **Statement.** Nine-point circle radius $= R/2$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="140" cy="112" r="80" fill="none" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="1.2"/><circle cx="140" cy="122" r="40" fill="rgba(180,83,9,0.10)" stroke="#b45309" stroke-width="1.4"/><polygon points="140,32 76,150 204,150" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0.85"/><circle cx="140" cy="150" r="3" fill="#16a34a"/><circle cx="172" cy="91" r="3" fill="#16a34a"/><circle cx="108" cy="91" r="3" fill="#16a34a"/><circle cx="140" cy="112" r="4" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="140" cy="122" r="4" fill="#b45309"/><circle cx="140" cy="32" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="76" cy="150" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="204" cy="150" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="135" y="24" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="62" y="162" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="210" y="162" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="148" y="114" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#2563eb">O</text><text x="148" y="127" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#b45309">N</text><text x="30" y="196" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">circumcircle (gray, radius R) + nine-point circle (orange, radius R/2)</text></svg>

steps: **The nine points.** (1) midpoints of the three sides, (2) feet of the three altitudes, (3) midpoints of $HA, HB, HC$. All nine lie on the same circle.
**Where it sits.** Center $N$ is the midpoint of $OH$ (the nine-point center). Radius is $R/2$.

**Why $R/2$?** Apply a homothety centered at $H$ with ratio $1/2$. It maps each vertex $A$ to the midpoint of $HA$ — a nine-point. It maps the circumcircle (passing through $A, B, C$) to a circle of half radius, passing through the three midpoints of $HA, HB, HC$. Some careful case-checking shows the same image circle also passes through the side midpoints and altitude feet — giving all nine points on a circle of radius $R/2$.

questions.prompt: Which collection of nine points does the 'nine-point circle' pass through?
choices: Three vertices, three side midpoints, three altitude feet
Three side midpoints, three altitude feet, and the three midpoints of HA, HB, HC (where H is the orthocenter)
Three vertices, three side midpoints, three angle-bisector feet
Three vertices and six random points
hints: The vertices themselves are NOT among the nine — they sit on the circumcircle (which is twice as large).
The nine points come in three groups of three: side midpoints, altitude feet, midpoints from H to each vertex.
questions.explanation: The nine points are: (1) the three midpoints of the sides; (2) the three feet of the altitudes; (3) the three midpoints of the segments from each vertex to the orthocenter $H$. Discovered piece-by-piece by Euler, Brianchon, and Poncelet in the 1820s.
steps: **Three groups of three points.** Group A: midpoints of sides $BC, CA, AB$. Group B: feet of altitudes from $A, B, C$. Group C: midpoints of $HA, HB, HC$ (segments from each vertex to the orthocenter).
**All nine on one circle.** Centered at $N$ = midpoint of $OH$, radius $R/2$.
**Why is it remarkable?** Three a-priori unrelated 'systems of three' all sit on the same circle. It's one of the most surprising results of classical Euclidean geometry.

hints: Use $H = 3G - 2O$.
questions.explanation: By the Euler vector identity: $\vec{H} = 3\vec{G} - 2\vec{O} = 3(2, 3) - 2(0, 0) = (6, 9)$. Sanity check: $|OG| = \sqrt{13}$, $|GH| = \sqrt{(6-2)^2 + (9-3)^2} = \sqrt{52} = 2\sqrt{13}$. Ratio $1:2$ ✓.
steps: **Use the Euler identity.** $\vec{H} = 3\vec{G} - 2\vec{O}$.
**Verify the ratio.** $|OG| = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13}$. $|GH| = \sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{52} = 2\sqrt{13}$. Ratio $OG : GH = 1 : 2$ ✓ — and $O, G, H$ are collinear on $y = 1.5\,x$.

questions.promptTex: \text{For any triangle, } |AH| = 2 \cdot OM_a, \\ \text{where } M_a \text{ is the midpoint of side } BC \text{ (opposite } A\text{)}. \\ \text{If } OM_a = 7, \text{ find } |AH|.
hints: Just apply the identity: $|AH| = 2 \cdot OM_a$.
questions.explanation: Classical identity: $|AH| = 2 \cdot OM_a$, where $OM_a$ is the perpendicular distance from the circumcenter to side $a$. (Both $OM_a$ and $AH$ point in the same direction, since both are perpendicular to $BC$.) So $|AH| = 2 \cdot 7 = 14$.
steps: **Statement.** $|AH| = 2 \cdot OM_a$ — distance from vertex to orthocenter equals twice the distance from circumcenter to opposite side. Mirror identities at $B, C$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="140,30 40,170 230,170" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="140" y1="30" x2="140" y2="105" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.8"/><line x1="135" y1="170" x2="135" y2="135" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.8"/><circle cx="140" cy="105" r="4" fill="#16a34a"/><circle cx="135" cy="135" r="4" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="135" cy="170" r="3" fill="#475569"/><circle cx="140" cy="30" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="40" cy="170" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="230" cy="170" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="135" y="22" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="26" y="185" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="236" y="185" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="148" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#16a34a">H</text><text x="118" y="132" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#2563eb">O</text><text x="122" y="185" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">M_a</text><text x="148" y="70" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">AH</text><text x="116" y="160" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb" font-style="italic">OM_a</text></svg>

steps: **Plug in.** $|AH| = 2 \cdot 7 = 14$.

**Why is the identity true?** Vector form: $\vec{AH} = 2 \vec{OM_a}$. Both vectors are perpendicular to $BC$ and point in the same direction. From the Euler relation $\vec{OH} = \vec{OA} + \vec{OB} + \vec{OC}$ (an equivalent form), subtracting $\vec{OA}$ gives $\vec{OH} - \vec{OA} = \vec{OB} + \vec{OC} = 2\vec{OM_a}$ (since $M_a$ is the midpoint of $BC$). And $\vec{OH} - \vec{OA} = \vec{AH}$. So $\vec{AH} = 2\vec{OM_a}$.
**Pattern lesson.** Vertex-to-orthocenter distances and circumcenter-to-side distances are tied in a perfect 2:1 ratio. Many olympiad problems exploit this.
